_work.title: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
_work.creator: various (attributed to Solomon, Apollonius of Tyana, and others)
_work.place: England, France, Germany, Italy, Byzantine Empire
_work.rights: Public domain / CC BY / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The *Ars Notoria* is the most widely copied magical manual of the medieval learned tradition — a system of angelic theurgy, concentrated prayer, and visual meditation on the *notae*: extraordinary circular, star-shaped, polygonal, and interlaced diagrams inscribed with divine names, Hebrew letters, *verba ignota*, and schematic figures. Practitioners believed that contemplating these images while reciting the accompanying orations would secure direct infusion of knowledge from the seven liberal arts, granted by angels sent by Solomon. First attested in the early 13th century and condemned by Thomas Aquinas, the tradition fed directly into John of Morigny's *Livre des figures*, the *Sworn Book of Honorius* (with its *Sigillum Dei*), and eventually John Dee's Enochian system. This grouping collects the core *Ars Notoria* notae alongside their cognate traditions: the Islamic *Shams al-Ma'ārif* (al-Būnī's Arabic parallel), the Hebrew *Sefer Raziel*, the Byzantine *Hygromanteia*, the Solomonic conjuration diagrams, and the 18th–19th century popular grimoire inheritors.

entries.title: First Figure of Grammar (*Ars Notoria*), BnF Latin 9336
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (French scribe)
entries.summary:
The first figure of grammar from the *Ars Notoria* — the singular, extraordinary visual instrument of the most widely copied magical learning text of the medieval West — held in BnF Latin 9336, one of the earliest surviving manuscripts of the tradition.

## Iconography
The *nota* is a densely constructed diagram of interlacing bands, concentric rings, and compartments filled with divine names, Hebrew-derived letter-strings, and *verba ignota* — 'unknown words' whose precise pronunciation was believed to carry operative force. This figure corresponds to the art of grammar: correct speech, right understanding, the ground of all literacy. Its visual complexity is not decorative but functional; the practitioner was to gaze upon it for an extended time while reciting the accompanying orations, allowing the discipline to be infused directly into the mind by angelic intermediaries.

## Significance
The *Ars Notoria* (Art of Notes, or Notarial Art) circulated from at least the early 13th century, often attributed to Solomon or Apollonius of Tyana, and claimed to convey the seven liberal arts, memory, eloquence, and divine wisdom through prayer and visual contemplation rather than ordinary study. Thomas Aquinas condemned it as trafficking with demons; John of Salisbury described it in the 12th century; John of Morigny spent years in visionary dialogue reworking it. Its imagery belongs to the tradition of figure-magic: the diagram as channel rather than illustration. BnF Latin 9336 is one of the key witnesses; Julien Véronèse's 2007 critical edition is the standard scholarly treatment.

## For artists & game designers
A 'study figure' the player activates to unlock a skill — knowledge literally written into the mind by an angelic patron, bypassing the ordinary grind of learning.

entries.title: Solomon Receives the *Ars Notoria* from the Angel Pamphilius
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (French or Italian scribe)
entries.summary:
Solomon receives the *Ars Notoria* from the angel Pamphilius in the Temple — the founding scene of the tradition, in which divine learning passes directly from the angelic realm into human hands.

## Iconography
The image shows a crowned Solomon figure in dialogue with a winged angel, the exchange framed within a temple or palace setting. The angel carries or presents the book — the *Ars Notoria* itself — to the king, establishing its claim to revealed rather than merely human authorship. The hieratic composition deliberately mirrors scenes of scriptural revelation: the gesture and setting echo images of Moses receiving the Law, grounding magical authority in the same vocabulary as sacred tradition.

## Significance
The Solomonic attribution was the standard medieval legitimating device for ritual magic: by claiming derivation from Solomon's own angelic instruction, a text placed itself in the lineage of God's most favoured operative. The angel Pamphilius is unique to the *Ars Notoria* tradition and appears in the prologues of several manuscripts as the celestial transmitter of the figures and orations. This image from BnF Latin 7153 (15th–16th c.) is one of the most evocative surviving frontispieces of the tradition. The *Ars Notoria* was not condemned as diabolical but as problematically circumventing human effort — the scandal was that it worked by divine infusion, not demonic pact.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive 'forbidden gift' scene: a patron-angel privately delivering a system of power, outside the ordinary channels of study, church, or guild.

entries.title: Figure of Logic / Dialectic (*Ars Notoria*), Bodleian MS Bodley 951
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Haplo1990 (reconstruction after Bodleian MS Bodley 951, f. 10v)
entries.date: Original tradition 12th–13th c.; this reconstruction 21st c.
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (reconstruction by Haplo1990). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The figure of logic (*nota logicae vel dialecticae*) from the *Ars Notoria*, reconstructed from Bodleian MS Bodley 951 — one of the most structurally elaborate of the surviving notae.

## Iconography
The diagram presents the characteristic *nota* structure: a central form — here corresponding to the art of logic or dialectic — composed of interlacing lines, barred bands, and lettered compartments. The compartments carry the divine names and *verba ignota* associated with the angelic powers of reasoned argument and disputational skill. The interlace creates a visual density that is not decorative but mnemonic: the practitioner's gaze is meant to track, hold, and absorb the figure, not merely glance at it.

## Significance
MS Bodley 951 is one of the key English witnesses to the *Ars Notoria*. The Bodleian collection preserves several Ars Notoria manuscripts, reflecting the text's wide 13th–15th century English circulation in clerical and university circles. The scholar Julien Véronèse has catalogued the manuscript witnesses and established that the *notae* underwent significant evolution across transmission: figures were redrawn, simplified, elaborated, and occasionally corrupted. This reconstruction by a modern scholar working from the folio is therefore both a document about the tradition and an act of interpretation within it.

## For artists & game designers
The 'reasoning' skill glyph — unlock debate, persuasion, and deduction mechanics by contemplating this angular, interlaced diagram.

entries.title: Sigillum Dei Aemeth (Seal of God, True)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: John Dee and Edward Kelley (attr.); this rendering anonymous
entries.rights: CC0 (public domain dedication). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* — 'Seal of the True God' — the master protective and summoning diagram of John Dee's Enochian angelic system, descending directly from the *Liber Juratus* (Sworn Book of Honorius, 13th–14th c.).

## Iconography
The seal is a complex concentric diagram: outer rings carry the divine names and the names of angels governing the seven planets; an inner heptagram and heptagon are inscribed with further angel-names and divine attributes; a central pentagram or five-pointed figure lies at the heart. The whole is a cosmogram encoding the governance of creation through angelic hierarchies — to possess and correctly employ the seal is to operate within the exact structure of celestial order.

## Significance
The *Sigillum Dei* descends from the *Liber Juratus*, where it is presented as the seal by which God governs angels and devils — the master instrument of the Honorian tradition, which is itself the direct heir of the *Ars Notoria* current. John Dee received this version of the seal in 1582 through Edward Kelley, who perceived it during a crystal-gazing session; Dee then had seven copies made in beeswax, placed beneath the legs of his scrying table and his chair. The British Museum preserves Dee's original wax seals. The seal became the central instrument of the Enochian sessions, linking the *Ars Notoria* → *Liber Juratus* → Dee lineage directly.

## For artists & game designers
The master ward of the Western magical tradition: place beneath a workspace to establish a protected ritual environment; nine-fold ring structure makes it visually unmistakable as a 'high tier' seal.

entries.title: Sigillum Dei (Giorgio Anselmi / Georgius Parmensis)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Giorgio Anselmi (Georgius Parmensis), 15th c.
entries.summary:
A version of the *Sigillum Dei* (Seal of God) by Giorgio Anselmi (Georgius Parmensis) — one of the most formidable diagrams of the medieval learned-magic tradition, parent of John Dee's *Sigillum Dei Aemeth*.

## Iconography
The seal is built from nested geometry: an outer ring carrying the names of God and his angels in Latin and pseudo-Hebrew, an inner heptagon or heptagram with angel-names in each vertex, and a pentagram or five-pointed star at the centre. Every zone is packed with divine names, crosses, and characters, each compartment assigned to a specific spiritual power. The overall effect is that of a sealed cosmogram: creation's governance structure rendered in geometry, with no space left unclaimed by divine authority.

## Significance
The *Sigillum Dei* descends from the *Liber Juratus Honorii* (Sworn Book of Honorius, compiled c. 1300) and was the central talisman of the Honorian tradition. Julien Véronèse and Jan R. Veenstra have traced its genealogy and noted its direct dependence on the *Ars Notoria* current — the *Liber Juratus* is, in many respects, an evolved and Christianized redaction of the Notoria. Giorgio Anselmi (fl. 15th c., Parma) was one of the learned Italian practitioners who copied and refined these instruments; his version is notable for its precision and textual richness.

## For artists & game designers
The 'old master' version of the Sigillum — a densely architectural seal suggesting generational accumulation, the protective mandala that has been refined across centuries.

entries.title: John Dee's Angelic Operations Notebook (Sloane MS 3188)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: John Dee
entries.summary:
A page from John Dee's own record of his angelic scrying operations, British Library Sloane MS 3188 — the primary document of the Enochian system and the culmination of the *Ars Notoria* → *Liber Juratus* lineage.

## Iconography
The manuscript page shows Dee's meticulous record of an angelic session: arrangements of the instruments (the showstone, the Table of Practice, the Sigillum Dei Aemeth), notation of the spirit communications received through Edward Kelley's visions in the crystal, and the careful logging of angelic names and utterances. The shaky minuscule hand is Dee's own; the record exists as a real-time transcript of an event he believed was literally occurring.

## Significance
Sloane MS 3188 is, alongside the Sloane 3189 and British Library Cotton MSS, the foundational primary source for Dee's Enochian system. Dee understood his sessions as a direct continuation of the Solomonic-Honorian tradition of angelic communication: the *Sigillum Dei Aemeth* beneath his scrying table was the Honorian instrument; the *Liber Juratus* was in his library; the *Ars Notoria* framework of angelic knowledge transmission is the template his system inhabits. Deborah Harkness's *John Dee's Conversations with Angels* and György Szönyi's *John Dee's Occultism* are the standard modern studies.

## For artists & game designers
The working mage's notebook: handwritten session logs, crossed-out errors, careful diagrams — knowledge hard-won in real time from an unreliable supernatural channel.

entries.title: Sefer Raziel HaMalakh — Page of Protective Amulet-Figures (*Segulot*)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (Jewish scribe)
entries.date: Medieval; this image from later printed or ms copy
entries.place: Ashkenazi Jewish tradition (Eastern Europe or Germany)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: check Commons provenance for exact MS or edition]
entries.summary:
A page of *segulot* (protective amulet-diagrams) from the *Sefer Raziel HaMalakh* — the Book of the Angel Raziel, the Hebrew parallel to the Latin *Ars Notoria* tradition in its claim to angelic transmission of universal knowledge.

## Iconography
The page presents rows of circular or square figures inscribed with Hebrew divine names, angelic names, and Psalm verses — the classic *segulot* format. Each figure is keyed to a specific protective or remedial purpose: easing childbirth, warding fire, healing illness, guarding sleep. They were copied onto parchment and worn on the body or hung in the home, the inscribed names themselves acting as the operative force.

## Significance
The *Sefer Raziel*, attributed to the angel Raziel who gave the book to Adam, is a composite work gathering Hekhalot, practical Kabbalah, and medieval learned-magic material. A Latin translation, the *Liber Razielis*, was assembled at the court of Alfonso X of Castile in the 13th century — contemporaneous with the Latin *Ars Notoria* — and circulated widely in learned European circles. Sophie Page's *Magic in the Cloister* documents its presence in English monastic libraries. The parallel traditions — Latin *Ars Notoria* and Hebrew *Sefer Raziel* — drew on overlapping networks of divine-name magic and reflect the shared intellectual world of Jewish and Christian learned magic in medieval Europe.

## For artists & game designers
A page of ready-made portable charms: copy the figure onto parchment, wear it — the magic is in the inscription, not the practitioner.

entries.title: Sefer Raziel HaMalakh — Diagram Leaf (*Brockhaus and Efron Jewish Encyclopedia*)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Brockhaus and Efron (editors)
entries.summary:
A diagram from the *Sefer Raziel HaMalakh* as reproduced in the Brockhaus and Efron Jewish Encyclopedia (1906) — a scholarly record of the angelic amulet tradition in print.

## Iconography
The image reproduces a page from the *Sefer Raziel*, showing the characteristic format of Hebrew amulet-figures: inscribed diagrams organized on a page, each combining geometric shapes with divine names, angelic names, and biblical verses to form portable operational talismans. The encyclopedia context frames these as documents of Jewish religious folklore and mysticism.

## Significance
The Brockhaus and Efron Jewish Encyclopedia (1906–13, in Russian) was the first major modern scholarly encyclopedia of Jewish culture and religion, and its reproductions of manuscript materials — including *Sefer Raziel* diagrams — made these traditions visible to a scholarly and secular audience for the first time at scale. The image represents the intersection of the *Sefer Raziel* tradition with early 20th-century academic treatment of Jewish practical mysticism, anticipating Gershom Scholem's subsequent scholarship. It documents the long reach of the *Ars Notoria* parallel: divine-name diagrams as a cross-cultural phenomenon shared by Latin and Hebrew learned magic.

## For artists & game designers
A scholarly plate of 'comparative magical diagrams' — the academic classification of real operative objects, useful for in-game lore-book aesthetics.

entries.title: *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* — Title Page (allegedly 1760)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: check Commons for institution or edition]
entries.summary:
The title page of the *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* — the notorious French ceremonial-magic manual that exploited the authority of the *Liber Juratus Honorii* (Sworn Book of Honorius) while transforming its angelic theurgy into demonic conjuration.

## Iconography
The title page presents the classic 18th-century French grimoire format: an ornate or plain title announcing papal authorship, the date of publication (1760 is claimed; scholars place actual composition later), and the authority of Pope Honorius III. The attribution to a pope was itself the scandal — and the draw. The design establishes the text's claim within the Solomonic-Honorian lineage while inverting its original purpose.

## Significance
The *Liber Juratus Honorii* (Sworn Book, c. 1300) was a deeply Christian text centred on the beatific vision and angelic authority; the *Ars Notoria* was condemned but not diabolical. The 18th-century *Grimoire du Pape Honorius* reversed this entirely: using the Honorian name to authorize a demonic conjuration manual, it represents the final transformation of the clerical angelic-theurgy current into popular diablerie. The grimoire instructs priests in how to abuse the Mass to summon and bind demons — a deliberate inversion of the Honorian liturgical magic. Arthur Waite and Collin de Plancy both described and partially reproduced it. The connection to the *Ars Notoria* is genealogical: Honorius I, Honorius III, and the unnamed compiler of the *Liber Juratus* form one chain; this grimoire parasitizes that chain.

## For artists & game designers
A 'corrupt grimoire' prop — the same name and lineage as a holy text, now repurposed for diabolical ends; the cover suggests authority, the contents betray it.

entries.title: Magical Circles and Seals of Solomon — 19th Century Grimoire Plate
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (occultist)
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Four diagrams of magical circles and Solomonic seals from a 19th-century grimoire manuscript, held in the Wellcome Collection — the working instruments of Solomonic conjuration as they circulated in the occult revival.

## Iconography
The plate shows four separate figures: a protective circle of Solomon inscribed with divine names in Hebrew and Latin, a Triangle of Solomon into which the summoned spirit is compelled to appear, smaller Solomonic seals, and a vessel or container form. Each is laid out as a practical working diagram — the kind drawn on the floor in chalk, inscribed on parchment, or engraved on wax at the planetary hour.

## Significance
The Wellcome Collection holds an extraordinary archive of occult manuscripts and printed books reflecting European ritual magic from the medieval period through the 19th century. This 19th-century plate documents the continued use and copying of Solomonic conjuration diagrams well into the era of the occult revival, bridging manuscript culture and the printed editions of Mathers and Waite. The protective circle descends directly from the *Heptameron*, the *Clavicula Salomonis*, and ultimately from the spatial-purification principles of the *Ars Notoria* tradition, where the practitioner's space is separated from the ordinary world before angelic contact is attempted.

## For artists & game designers
A complete set of ritual-space blueprints: protective circle, summoning triangle, seals — the three-component system of Solomonic operations depicted together on one plate.

entries.title: Fragment of the *Picatrix* (*Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm*) Manuscript
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (attributed to Maslama al-Majriti, 10th–11th c.)
entries.summary:
A fragment of the *Picatrix* (*Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm*, 'Aim of the Sage') — the foundational handbook of astral image-magic, the Islamic parallel to the *Ars Notoria* current and its greatest influence on Renaissance learned magic.

## Iconography
The manuscript leaf presents dense Arabic text — the operational instructions of the *Picatrix*: the manufacture of talismans at elected astrological moments, the carving of planetary and decan images on appropriate metals, the suffumigations and petitions addressed to planetary spirits. The material is technical and precise: times, materials, figures, prayers — a surgical manual for drawing celestial virtue into matter.

## Significance
Compiled in al-Andalus c. 10th–11th century and translated into Castilian and then Latin under Alfonso X of Castile in 1256, the *Picatrix* transmitted Hermetic, Neoplatonic, and Sabian (Harranian) astral magic into European learned culture. Ficino's *De vita coelitus comparanda*, Agrippa's *De occulta philosophia*, and the planetary-talisman chapter of the *Clavicula Salomonis* all descend from the *Picatrix*. David Pingree's edition of both the Arabic (*Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm*) and the Latin (*Picatrix*) texts is the standard reference. Unlike the *Ars Notoria*, the *Picatrix* explicitly describes a mechanical theory — the image carved at the right stellar moment captures the star's influence — rather than the Notoria's relational-angelic model, but both share the project of guaranteed knowledge and power through correct procedure.

## For artists & game designers
The 'craft under the stars' mechanic: talisman creation requiring astrological timing, correct materials, and inscribed planetary figures — the *Picatrix* is the original source.

entries.title: *Shams al-Ma'ārif al-Kubrā* (The Great Sun of Knowledge) — al-Būnī
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Ahmad ibn Ali al-Buni (d. 1225 CE)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 igo (Khalili Collections). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A double spread from al-Būnī's *Shams al-Ma'ārif al-Kubrā* (The Great Sun of Knowledge) — the supreme Arabic manual of divine-name magic, letter mysticism, and *wafq* (magic squares), a direct Islamic parallel to the Latin *Ars Notoria*.

## Iconography
The spread shows the characteristic al-Būnī page: dense Arabic calligraphy interleaved with talismanic figures, *wafq* (Arabic numerical magic squares aligned with divine names), geometric diagrams, and rows of inscribed panels. The aesthetic is one of total inscription — no space is dead, every corner carries operative text. The figures combine the visual vocabulary of Islamic geometric ornament with the functional requirements of operative magic.

## Significance
Ahmad ibn Ali al-Būnī (Egypt, d. 1225 CE) wrote the *Shams al-Ma'ārif* contemporaneously with the earliest Latin *Ars Notoria* manuscripts — the two traditions share the moment. Al-Būnī's system centres on *ism al-a'zam* (the greatest name of God), letter combinations (*tarkīb*), and *wafq* magic squares as instruments of divine access and operative power, paralleling the *Ars Notoria*'s divine names and *notae* almost point for point. The *Shams al-Ma'ārif* was the most widely copied Arabic occult text; it circulated across the Islamic world from Morocco to Indonesia and fed into the Ottoman and Persian occult traditions. Liana Saif and Noah Gardiner have written on al-Būnī's work; this Khalili manuscript is a high-quality witness.

## For artists & game designers
The Arabic tradition's master diagram-book: dense, geometric, inscribed — visually distinct from the Latin notae but functionally identical in premise: operate on divine names through correct figures.

entries.title: Circle of Solomon and Triangle of Solomon (*Lemegeton*, 1904 Mathers-Crowley edition)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley (eds.)
entries.summary:
The Circle of Solomon and Triangle of Solomon from the *Ars Goetia* (Lemegeton), in the 1904 Mathers-Crowley edition — the canonical printed rendering of the Solomonic conjuration space.

## Iconography
The plate shows two figures: the Circle of Solomon, a triple-ringed protective space inscribed with divine names (including the Tetragrammaton, Adonai, and the names of the archangels Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, and Uriel), with a central pentagram; and the Triangle of Solomon, a smaller triangular figure placed outside the circle into which the summoned spirit is compelled to appear. The operator stands within the circle; the spirit manifests in the triangle.

## Significance
The Circle-and-Triangle system is the spatial grammar of Solomonic demonology and descends ultimately from the *Heptameron* and the various *Clavicula* manuscripts. Mathers and Crowley's 1904 *Goetia* standardized these diagrams for the modern occult world. The principle — an inscribed space of divine names separating the operator from the summoned entity — is structurally identical to the *Ars Notoria* practitioner's purified ritual space, but repurposed for coercive rather than petitionary magic. The contrast encapsulates the difference between the traditions: the *Ars Notoria* petitions angels with prayer; the Goetia commands demons with seals and a threatening spatial grammar.

## For artists & game designers
The definitive summoning-space diagram: a circle for the player (protected), a triangle for the summoned entity (constrained). This layout is the archetypal 'boss fight arena with rules'.

entries.title: *The Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* — Frontispiece (1880 edition)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (attributed to Moses; this ed. 1880)
motifs: sixth and seventh books of moses
key_concepts: Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses
entries.summary:
The frontispiece of *The Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* in its 1880 edition — one of the most widely distributed folk-magic grimoires of the German and German-American tradition, placing itself in direct succession to the biblical Moses.

## Iconography
The frontispiece shows the title, the attribution to Moses, and emblematic imagery establishing the work's claimed Mosaic-Solomonic authority. The design is characteristic of 19th-century occult popular publishing: authoritative title, Mosaic or mystical emblem, and a dateline suggesting antiquity.

## Significance
*The Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* is an 18th–19th century German compilation presenting itself as the secret books that Moses used to perform the plagues and miracles, now supplemented with Solomonic seals and protective charms. It circulated widely among German-speaking immigrant communities in North America (where it fed directly into Pennsylvania Dutch *Pow-wow* folk magic) and in West Africa (where German missionaries and traders introduced it, and it was absorbed into various African and African-diaspora magical traditions, including in Jamaica and Nigeria). The attribution to Moses parallels the Solomonic attribution of the *Ars Notoria* cycle: both invoke biblical authority to legitimate extra-ecclesiastical magical practice.

## For artists & game designers
The immigrant's grimoire: a folk-magic book carried across the Atlantic, absorbed into new cultures, and adapted for new needs — a prop with a genuinely complex historical journey.

entries.title: *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* — Mosaic Magic Symbols Plate (Fig. 104, 1880)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (ed. 1880, after older tradition)
key_concepts: Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses
entries.summary:
A plate of magical symbols from *The Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* (1880 edition) — the operative seals and figures of the Mosaic magical tradition as it was presented to 19th-century readers.

## Iconography
The figure presents a collection of talismanic signs and symbols described as originating with Moses: geometric shapes, star forms, ringed characters, and name-combinations. The style is simpler and more schematic than the medieval Solomonic pentacles or the *Ars Notoria* notae, reflecting the folk-magic context — these are meant to be copied by ordinary users without specialist training.

## Significance
The seals and symbols of the *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* represent the 19th-century popular end of the Solomonic-Mosaic magical tradition — vastly simplified from the learned Latin MS culture of the *Ars Notoria* cycle, but continuous with it in the key claim: that certain diagrams inscribed with divine names carry operative force when correctly reproduced. The book's rapid spread through Afro-Atlantic communities (particularly in Ghana, Nigeria, Jamaica, and southern United States) created one of the most geographically distributed folk-magic traditions of the modern era. George Eaton Simpson and Emmanuel Akyeampong have documented its reception in West Africa.

## For artists & game designers
Folk-level magical symbols: simpler and more accessible than learned seals, but drawing on the same tradition — the 'common person's grimoire' aesthetic.

entries.title: *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* — Fourth Seal
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (ed. 1880, after older tradition)
key_concepts: Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses
entries.summary:
The Fourth Seal from *The Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* (1880 edition) — a representative individual seal from the Mosaic magical compendium.

## Iconography
A circular or ringed figure, inscribed with names and characters, designed as a complete operative talisman. The 'Fourth Seal' designation locates it within the systematic enumeration of seals across the book — each seal corresponds to a specific operation or day. The visual vocabulary is consistent with the broader Solomonic seal tradition but rendered in the simplified style of the folk-magic print market.

## Significance
The individual seals of the *Sixth and Seventh Books of Moses* are functionally direct descendants of the *Clavicula Salomonis* pentacles and, through them, of the overall Solomonic-Honorian tradition. The simplification from elaborately inscribed manuscript pentacles to printed single-purpose seals reflects the democratization of learned magic across the 18th and 19th centuries: the same operative logic (divine names in prescribed geometric form), stripped of its Latin scholarly apparatus and made reproducible by ordinary people. In African and African-diaspora contexts, these seals were often further reinterpreted and syncretized with local spiritual practices.

## For artists & game designers
A clean individual seal tile — assign one effect per seal, let the player collect them as a set; the '4th' designation implies a progression or rotation system.

entries.title: Magical Alphabets Plate — Francis Barrett, *The Magus* (1801)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Francis Barrett
entries.summary:
The magical alphabets plate from Francis Barrett's *The Magus* (1801) — the Regency occult revival's systematic presentation of the cipher alphabets used to inscribe divine names, angelic names, and talismanic characters.

## Iconography
The plate presents several alternate magical scripts in parallel rows: the Celestial (Angelic) Alphabet, the Malachim script, the Passing the River alphabet, possibly the Theban script, and others — each a distinct system for encoding Hebrew, Latin, or angelic names in unreadable (to the uninitiated) form. The plate is designed as a reference tool: a key for encoding and decoding the names inscribed on seals, lamens, and talismans.

## Significance
Barrett's *The Magus* (1801) was the first major English-language synthesis of the Agrippa-Paracelsian tradition since the 17th century, drawing heavily on Cornelius Agrippa's *Three Books of Occult Philosophy* and transmitting it to the 19th-century occult revival. The magical alphabets derive from Agrippa's *Third Book* (1533), which in turn adapted them from manuscript sources. The alphabets are directly connected to the *Ars Notoria* and *Liber Juratus* tradition: the divine names in the notae and seals were often encoded in these scripts as a mark of esoteric restriction. Barrett's plate became the standard modern reference and was used by the Golden Dawn and subsequent practitioners.

## For artists & game designers
A complete 'cipher alphabet' reference plate: six distinct scripts for encoding the same underlying names, useful for in-game cryptography, runic-style inscriptions, or collectible 'key' items.

entries.title: *Dreifacher Höllenzwang* (Triple Hell-Compulsion) — Title Page
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (attributed to Dr. Faust)
entries.summary:
The title page of the *Dreifacher Höllenzwang* (Triple Hell-Compulsion) — one of the most widely circulated German magical handbooks of the 18th–19th centuries, claiming Faustian authority for its demonic conjuration procedures.

## Iconography
The title page announces 'threefold compulsion of Hell', attributed to the legendary Dr. Faust — the quintessential German magician-figure whose pact with the Devil anchors the compulsion claim. The layout is typical of early-modern German magic pamphlets: dramatic title, authoritative attribution, and the promise of reliable operational control over spirits.

## Significance
The *Höllenzwang* tradition (compulsion of Hell) represents the German popular end of the broader Solomonic coercive-magic spectrum, complementing the learned *Clavicula* tradition while reaching a much wider audience. Attribution to Faust replaced the learned Solomon/Moses/Agrippa attributions with a specifically German Protestant-cultural figure — the scholar who went too far — and this gave the texts a distinct domestic resonance. They circulated in cheap printed pamphlets and manuscript copies across German-speaking Europe and were consulted by practitioners who could not access the Latin *Clavicula* manuscripts. The *Höllenzwang* literature is documented by Karl Kiesewetter and Frank Baron in the scholarship on German occult literature.

## For artists & game designers
The 'forbidden pamphlet' prop: a cheap, populist version of high grimoire culture, associated with the Faust legend and the dangers of overreaching — a much more accessible aesthetic than the ornate Latin MS tradition.

entries.title: A Theurgist Performing Rituals to Exercise Divine Powers — Wellcome Collection
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (coloured aquatint, early 19th c.)
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0 (Wellcome Collection). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A coloured aquatint depicting a theurgist in the midst of a ritual to exercise divine powers — a rare early 19th-century image of the magical practitioner at work, held in the Wellcome Collection.

## Iconography
The image shows a robed figure operating within or around a ritual space — likely a magic circle, with implements of the art (candles, wand, book, diagram). The 'theurgist' label is significant: theurgy, literally 'god-working', was the specific term for ritual magic aimed at divine contact as opposed to lower-order sorcery or demon-conjuration. The coloured aquatint format suggests this is an illustration from an occult or antiquarian publication of the period rather than a working diagram.

## Significance
The Wellcome Collection holds the largest archive of the history of medicine and occult practice in the UK, and images like this one document the visual culture of magical practice in the early 19th century — the period of the occult revival that bridges the Enlightenment dismissal of magic and the Romantic recuperation of it. The theurgist image belongs to the same cultural moment as Barrett's *Magus* (1801) and prefigures the Golden Dawn systematization of 'high magic' as theurgy. The term itself, revived from Iamblichus and Proclus, was used deliberately to distinguish ritual magic aimed at divine/angelic ascent — like the *Ars Notoria* — from mere sorcery.

## For artists & game designers
A rare practitioner scene rather than diagram — shows the human operator in action: robed, centred, working the space. Useful for NPC or player-character magical-practice aesthetics.

entries.title: *The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abra-Melin the Mage* — Title Page (1898)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: S. L. MacGregor Mathers (trans.); original attributed to Abraham of Worms
entries.summary:
The title page of S. L. MacGregor Mathers's 1898 English translation of *The Book of the Sacred Magic of Abra-Melin the Mage* — the most influential medieval Jewish-magic text to enter the Western occult revival.

## Iconography
The title page announces the text and its editor, establishing the work's authority: a 15th-century instruction for attaining the 'Knowledge and Conversation of the Holy Guardian Angel' after eighteen months of purification, followed by the binding of all demons to the operator's will. The presentation is that of a scholarly occult text — Mathers positions it as historical translation rather than invention, lending it the authority of antiquity.

## Significance
The *Book of Abramelin* (original c. 1458, attributed to Abraham ben Simon of Worms, drawing on an encounter with the Egyptian mage Abramelin) describes an extended operation explicitly parallel to the *Ars Notoria* premise: a sustained period of purification and prayer leading to direct angelic contact, which then conveys universal knowledge and power. Where the *Ars Notoria* works through gazing at notae while reciting orations, Abramelin works through a daily prayer regimen culminating in the HGA's arrival. Mathers's translation made it available to Aleister Crowley, for whom the Abramelin Operation became the central project of his magical career and the axis of the *A∴A∴* system. Georg Dehn and Steven Guth's critical edition (2006) works from older manuscript sources than Mathers had.

## For artists & game designers
The long ritual's end-state: 18 months of discipline leading to the permanent angelic patron — a slow-burn narrative structure built into the magical system itself.

entries.title: *Geistlicher Schild* (Spiritual Shield) — German Protective Charm Broadsheet
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.summary:
The *Geistlicher Schild* (Spiritual Shield) — a German protective charm broadsheet invoking divine and angelic names to ward harm from the owner — the popular printed incarnation of the *Ars Notoria*'s operative principle.

## Iconography
The broadsheet presents a structured protective charm: the name of Christ, divine names (often the Tetragrammaton in Latin transliteration), angelic names, and scriptural verses arranged in a visual pattern. The shield metaphor is operative: the text-and-image configuration is meant to function as physical armour against evil, illness, violence, and supernatural attack. Such broadsheets were displayed on walls, carried in pockets, and placed in cradles.

## Significance
The *Geistlicher Schild* tradition represents the popular-print endpoint of the divine-name magic that the *Ars Notoria* exemplified in its most elaborate learned form. The key operative claim is identical: that certain arrangements of divine names, correctly inscribed and deployed, carry power. What differs is the register: learned Latin manuscript ritual versus cheap German print charm. The tradition connects to the *Schutzbrief* (protection letter), the *SATOR* square, and the body of German Lutheran folk-magic that was never fully separated from the Protestant official culture. Keith Thomas's *Religion and the Decline of Magic* and Hildegard Krekeler's studies of German protective charms are relevant.

## For artists & game designers
A 'passive ward' item: the protective broadsheet posted on the door or carried in the belt — not activated by ritual but by correct inscription. An everyday magical prop.

entries.title: Byzantine Magic Manuscript — *Hygromanteia* (BL Harley MS 5596, cropped)
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine Greek scribe)
entries.summary:
A leaf from British Library Harley MS 5596, a 15th-century Byzantine Greek manuscript of the *Hygromanteia* or *Magical Treatise of Solomon* — the Greek-language bridge between late-antique magic and the Western Solomonic grimoire tradition.

## Iconography
The manuscript page shows the characteristic Byzantine magical manuscript format: Greek text interspersed with magical seals (*characteres*), spirit-signs, and ritual instructions for conjuring and binding the angels and spirits of the planetary hours. The dense small-hand calligraphy and the placement of the seals within the text reflect a working manual structure: read the instruction, perform the operation, use the seal depicted on the same page.

## Significance
Harley MS 5596 is one of the principal witnesses to the *Hygromanteia*, the Greek Solomonic handbook that Ioannis Marathakis has edited and translated as *The Magical Treatise of Solomon* (2011). The *Hygromanteia* belongs to a tradition of Byzantine Solomonic magic that predates the Latin *Clavicula* but fed into it, establishing the day-and-hour structure, planetary angel correspondences, and conjuration framework that the Latin tradition adopted. It is the Greek parallel to the same stream that produced the *Ars Notoria*: Solomonic-attributed texts of learned ritual magic aimed at angelic or spirit contact. The *characteres* in this manuscript closely resemble the notae of the *Ars Notoria* tradition — both are graphic instruments of spirit-address.

## For artists & game designers
The Eastern predecessor: the Greek manuscript aesthetics (uncial or minuscule hand, red-and-black ink, embedded spirit-signs) that underlies the Latin tradition, useful for establishing historical depth in a worldbuilding context.

entries.title: Byzantine Solomonic Manuscript, Full Page — BL Harley MS 5596
entries.work: Ars Notoria & the Angelic Theurgy Tradition
entries.creator: Anonymous (Byzantine Greek scribe)
entries.summary:
Full-page view of British Library Harley MS 5596 — the 15th-century Byzantine Greek *Hygromanteia* (*Magical Treatise of Solomon*), showing the complete manuscript layout of a Solomonic operational text.

## Iconography
The full page reveals the holistic structure of the Byzantine magical manuscript: dense Greek text in a careful minuscule hand, rubrication (red ink for titles and headings), and magical seals or figures embedded in the margins or between sections. The page is a complete unit of the ritual handbook — the text gives the oration, the seal provides the operative image, the rubric identifies the planetary hour or spiritual power being addressed.

## Significance
Viewing the full page of Harley MS 5596 reveals the manuscript as an object rather than merely a text-carrier: a carefully produced, well-preserved working manual of Byzantine Solomonic magic. The *Hygromanteia* was not a fringe text but a widely copied and used handbook; this manuscript's survival in the Harley Collection (assembled by Robert Harley, 1st Earl of Oxford, and donated to the British Museum in 1753) reflects the 18th-century collecting of occult manuscripts as objects of antiquarian interest. It is the most directly accessible Byzantine witness to the Greek-language tradition that shaped the Latin *Ars Notoria* milieu.

## For artists & game designers
The full manuscript aesthetic: Byzantine red-and-black layout, dense handwritten Greek, embedded operational seals — the 'foreign grimoire' or 'eastern magical tradition' visual register.
